Victory or Deathâ¢
On February 24, 1836, with the garrison surrounded and the Texan Army at the Alamo outnumbered, one of the most famous letters in American history was written by William B. Travis. It was addressed, âTo the People of Texas and All Americans in the World.â This letter was a passionate plea for aid for the Alamo garrison. He ended the letter âVictory or Deathâ â the only outcome this battle could have.

Commandancy of the The Alamo

Bejar, Feby. 24th. 1836

To the People of Texas & All Americans in the World-

Fellow Citizens & compatriots-

I am besieged, by a thousand or more of the Mexicans under Santa Anna - I have sustained a continual Bombardment & cannonade for 24 hours & have not lost a man - The enemy has demanded a surrender at discretion, otherwise, the garrison are to be put to the sword, if the fort is taken - I have answered the demand with a cannon shot, & our flag still waves proudly from the walls - I shall never surrender or retreat.  Then, I call on you in the name of Liberty, of patriotism & everything dear to the American character, to come to our aid, with all dispatch - The enemy is receiving reinforcements daily & will no doubt increase to three or four thousand in four or five days.  If this call is neglected, I am determined to sustain myself as long as possible & die like a soldier who never forgets what is due to his own honor & that of his country - Victory or Death.

William Barret Travis.

Lt. Col.comdt.

P. S.  The Lord is on our side - When the enemy appeared in sight we had not three bushels of corn - We have since found in deserted houses 80 or 90 bushels and got into the walls 20 or 30 head of Beeves.

Travis

From Google about sick missionaries:

Key points to remember when sick on an LDS mission:
Contact your mission leaders: This is crucial; they will advise you on the best course of action based on the severity of your illness and local protocols. 
Rest: Prioritize sleep and relaxation to aid in recovery. 
Take over-the-counter medications: Use approved medications as needed, following the instructions on the label. 
Seek medical attention if necessary: If your symptoms are severe or concerning, consult a local doctor or clinic immediately. 
Stay hydrated: Drink plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration.

Let me do a deep analysis of just 36 for you.

Deep Dive Analysis of Alma Chapter 36
This document provides a detailed analysis of Alma chapter 36, focusing on its renowned chiastic structure. This chapter is one of the most powerful and intricate examples of chiasmus in all of scripture, serving as Almaâs personal testimony to his son Helaman about his conversion.

Overall Context
Alma 36 is a flashback, a personal story told from a father to a son. Alma recounts his dramatic conversion experience to impress upon Helaman the reality of sin, the necessity of repentance, and the profound joy of redemption through Jesus Christ. The structure of the chapter is not merely decorative; it is the key to its meaning. The literary form mirrors the spiritual content, with the entire narrative pivoting on the moment Alma turns his thoughts to the Savior.

The Chiastic Structure of Alma 36
This chapter is a near-perfect, extended chiasmus. The mirrored ideas and phrases are laid out below, moving from the outer layers to the central turning point.

A (v. 1-2): The Commandment to Remember Godâs Deliverance

âMy son, give ear to my wordsâ¦ I would that ye should do as I have done, in remembering the captivity of our fathers; for they were in bondage, and none could deliver them except it were the God of Abrahamâ¦â
B (v. 3): The Promise of Support and Prosperity in the Land

ââ¦whosoever shall put their trust in God shall be supported in their trials, and their troubles, and their afflictions, and shall be lifted up at the last day.â
C (v. 4): Almaâs Testimony of Godâs Power

âI know that God is not mocked; for he that knoweth these things of which I have spoken, and doth not them, the same is not saved.â
D (v. 5): The Story of Almaâs Opposition to the Church

âI had been born of Godâ¦ I went about with the sons of Mosiah, seeking to destroy the church of Godâ¦â
E (v. 6-9): The Angelic Visitation and Confrontation

F (v. 10): Almaâs Physical Collapse and Inability to Speak

H (v. 12-13): The Torment of a Damned Soul

v. 16: âAnd it came to pass that as I was thus racked with torment, while I was harrowed up by the memory of my many sinsâ¦â

Conclusion: Structure as Theology
Alma 36 is the ultimate example of form mirroring content. The entire narrative structure forces the reader to the central pivot point: the moment Almaâs mind catches hold of the thought of Jesus Christ. Everything before that moment is a descent into darkness, pain, and a desire for annihilation. Everything after it is an ascent into light, joy, and a desire to bring others to that same joy.

The chiasmus is not just a literary device; it is a theological statement. It teaches that a life without Christ spirals inward toward a black hole of guilt and despair. The only way to reverse that spiral is to turn to the Savior. His name is the turning point of the chapter and the turning point of a life. The perfectly mirrored structure demonstrates the completeness of this transformationâthe joy is as profound as the pain was, the desire to build is as strong as the desire to destroy was, and the deliverance is as real as the bondage was.

The funniest story she told us (in her opinion) was when one of her brothers was 4 years old, he jumped straight into a pond trying to catch a butterfly with his net. Setsuko acted this out with a big jump which definitely surprised me considering she's 75 (I think). Spiritual thought
This week has been a week of huge progress as a companionship. Getting out and doing Missionary work feels so good. Things aren't perfect, and there's definitely times where we aren't using our Time 100 percent perfectly but it's way better than it has been. I can promise to each of you reading this that no matter what difficulties you are facing, God knows your trials and struggles. I have really tried to see things from his perspective recently, Maybe holding my tongue against my companion or waking him up if I don't want to ð (not happening all that often Fortunately) or following a prompting from the spirit. Things really seem much better as you are striving to Follow God's plan. It's different in each of our lives, so I'd invite you to pray and sincerely ask God what His plan looks like for you. Maybe even ask what you can do. I'm sure you'll be surprised by the response you get, I definitely have been.
